coredata.dc:title: Porphyrin derived dual-emissive carbon quantum dots: Customizable synthesis and application for intracellular Cu2+ quantification
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yuqian
Seidi, Farzad
Deng, Chao
Li, Ruyi
Xu, Tingting
Xiao, Huining
coredata.dc:description: Exploring sensory systems from metal ion receptors enables the continuous development of ion sensing technologies. Herein we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-sulfophenyl)porphyrin (TSPP) and additional carbon source, and its application in Cu2+ imaging and quantifying in living cells. The CQDs are synthesized via a hydrothermal method in a successive polymerization and carbonization process. Due to the surface traps introduced by TSPP residue, the CQDs are featured with dual-emissive property of green (505 nm) and red (682 nm) fluorescence. Moreover, the red-to-green fluorescence ratio is readily customizable by tuning the molar fraction of TSPP. Benefited from Cu2+ complexation induced red fluorescence quenching, the CQDs can be served as the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for Cu2+ detection with ultra-high sensitivity and excellent specificity. The nanosensor is demonstrated to linearly detect Cu2+ in the range of 0−50 nM, with an extremely low limit of detection (LOD) of 37 pM. More importantly, interference from other biological compositions, especially Fe3+, is successfully excluded via the site-selective coordination and specific electron transfer path. Based on the excellent analytical performance of the nanosensor, quantifying Cu2+ in living cells is realized according to the linear correlation between fluorescence ratio and intracellular Cu2+ content, which is parallelly detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). To demonstrate its practicality, the nanosensor is challenged to measure Cu2+ uptake kinetics for cell lines derived from different tissues.

head.ce:title: Porphyrin derived dual-emissive carbon quantum dots: Customizable synthesis and application for intracellular Cu2+ quantification

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring sensory systems from metal ion receptors enables the continuous development of ion sensing technologies. Herein we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-sulfophenyl)porphyrin (TSPP) and additional carbon source, and its application in Cu2+ imaging and quantifying in living cells. The CQDs are synthesized via a hydrothermal method in a successive polymerization and carbonization process. Due to the surface traps introduced by TSPP residue, the CQDs are featured with dual-emissive property of green (505 nm) and red (682 nm) fluorescence. Moreover, the red-to-green fluorescence ratio is readily customizable by tuning the molar fraction of TSPP. Benefited from Cu2+ complexation induced red fluorescence quenching, the CQDs can be served as the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for Cu2+ detection with ultra-high sensitivity and excellent specificity. The nanosensor is demonstrated to linearly detect Cu2+ in the range of 0−50 nM, with an extremely low limit of detection (LOD) of 37 pM. More importantly, interference from other biological compositions, especially Fe3+, is successfully excluded via the site-selective coordination and specific electron transfer path. Based on the excellent analytical performance of the nanosensor, quantifying Cu2+ in living cells is realized according to the linear correlation between fluorescence ratio and intracellular Cu2+ content, which is parallelly detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). To demonstrate its practicality, the nanosensor is challenged to measure Cu2+ uptake kinetics for cell lines derived from different tissues.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ranks the third in abundance among transition metals in human body, and is proved to be crucial for the function of several enzymes and proteins involved in a series of physiological processes [1]. Alterations in copper homeostasis are confirmed to be closely correlated with various disorders including genetic or neurodegenerative diseases, which are featured with copper deficiency or overload [2–4]. Based on the fact that copper distribution in different organs is not uniform and the uptake and release events are dynamic, to accurately and effectively sense the cellular concentration, as well as the cellular uptake kinetics of copper is of considerable significance for disease diagnosis and treatment. This has stimulated the drastic development of copper sensing technologies, among which the fluorescent sensing is the most promising one for their merits of high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and the possibility for in-situ imaging [5,6]. Therefore, a diverse range of fluorescent sensors based on organic dyes [7–9], noble metal nanomaterials [10–12], up-conversion nanoparticles [13,14], or quantum dots [15–17], have been devised for Cu2+ detection. Fluorescence of these sensors is adjustable relying on either Cu2+-receptor coordination [18] or Cu2+-dependent catalytic reactions [19] induced modulations of photo-induced electron transfer (PIET) [7,8,12], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [20], Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [10,13,15], or excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) [9]. Despite impressive results have been achieved, it is still a great challenge to quantify Cu2+ in complex biological media due to the interferences brought by: 1) other transition metal ions, such as Zn2+ and Fe3+, which show high tendency to be coordinated by the receptors for their similar ion size or electronic structure, with that of Cu2+ [21]. 2) Environmental parameters, instrumental efficiency, and sensor concentration. Although the massively developed ratiometric sensors provide more accurate Cu2+ detection results by integrating reference signals [22–26], they require sophisticated chemical or physical methods to combine multi-emissive fluorophores into one sensing system.

Porphyrins represent a significant family of macrocycle compounds which are vastly used for probing metal ions. In these compounds, four pyrroles are connected to each other through methylidine bridges, forming the porphin ring. They have a rigid planar structure with extended conjugated π-electron system, and a size-limited coordination cavity for complexing metal ions such as Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and Cu2+ [27,28]. Metalation of the free base porphyrins or their derivatives can give rise to their structural, as well as spectroscopic and electronic variations, making the detection of metal ions with high sensitivity possible [29–33]. For the Cu2+ detection instances, a distance-based paper analytical device was fabricated using porphyrin as a colorimetric reagent [34]. Color of the device is changed from green to pink due to the lowering of Q-band absorption of the porphyrin and a new peak at 541 nm appearing at the presence of Cu2+. With regard to a FRET system, transition of the absorption from porphyrin to Cu2+-porphyrin can effectively tune the FRET efficiency and thus change the fluorescence intensity of the sensor, as is the case for UCNPs/porphyrin nanokit [35]. Nevertheless, the determination of Cu2+ should be carried out in acidic media (pH ≤ 4.0) where porphyrins can selectively chelate Cu2+ and show certain absorption property to meet the requirement of visible color change or fluorescence resonance effect [34–36]. Moreover, binding of porphyrins and Cu2+ is kinetically slow unless the mixture is heated (temperature ≥ 70 ℃) [36,37]. Both conditions impede the application of porphyrin-based sensors for Cu2+ detection in living systems.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit a variety of advantages, such as excellent biocompatibility, photochemical stability as well as simple synthesis, over other fluorophores. The vast application of CQDs opens the new avenue of biological sensing and imaging. Recently, to modulate the emission color of CQDs is in the spotlight because their application can be further extended in multiplexed assays, white light-emitting diodes, or even anti-cancer [38–40]. Generally, the emission mechanism of CQDs can be categorized into three types: the band-gap emission from sp2 patterned π-domain (carbon core), surface trap-level states which are present in the band-gap, and individual molecule state emissions [41,42]. Supposing the predominant mechanism of a CQD is well determined, the emission color is facilely adjustable and can cover the whole visible light range. For the instance of CQDs with less surface traps, by changing the size of their conjugated π-domains, the emission color is expected to be modulated [43]. While for the those are rich in surface defect or molecular emission centers, the emission color can be regulated by changing surface oxidation degree or constitution of fluorescent molecules [44,45]. Although great progress has been made in the optical control of pure emission CQDs, to engineer the “multi-emission in one” CQDs is considered to be far more important and challenging, however, far less explored. The multi-emissive CQDs are appealing for being served as the ratiometric nanosensor without any post-treatment.

In this work, with the aim of quantifying Cu2+ in biological samples, we report the synthesis of dual-emissive carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-sulfophenyl)porphyrin (TSPP) and its application for Cu2+ detection with ultra-high sensitivity, accuracy, and responsive-rapidness. As shown in Scheme 1 , TSPP and additional carbon source (citric acid, CA) were hydrothermally treated to prepare the CQDs, which can simultaneously emit green fluorescence from the carbon core, and red fluorescence from the surface trap state introduced by TSPP residue. Moreover, the red-to-green fluorescence intensity ratio, as well as the morphologies and chemical properties of the CQDs could be readily customized by tuning the molar fraction of TSPP. With an appropriate CA amount, namely, TSPP-to-CA molar ratio of 1:50, the CQDs exhibit a red-to-green ratio of 2.2:1, which is extremely suitable for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Cu2+. Being used as the nanosensor, its red fluorescence is quenched in terms of the mechanism of PIET upon Cu2+ is avidly incorporated into the TSPP residue, while the green fluorescence remains constant, acting as an internal reference. Noticeably, the ratiometric fluorescent response of the nanosensor excludes the interferences from other transition metal ions or environmental factors, providing high detection accuracy. The advantages of the proposed CQD nanosensor allow to quantify Cu2+ in living cells, and further determine the Cu2+ uptake kinetics by different cell lines.

TSPP (≥ 95 %) and rhodamine 6 G (99 %) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid (99 %), CuSO4·5H2O (98 %), and FeCl3 were purchased from J&K chemical. The salts such as KCl, NaCl, ZnCl2 etc. were obtained from Aladdin chemical. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), Glucose (Glu), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and trypsin solution were provided by Fcmacs Biotech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Pall Cascade AN system.

The dual-emissive CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 5 μmol of TSPP and a certain amount of CA (from 25 to 750 μmol) were dissolved in 35 mL of pure water. After sonication for 5 min, the solution was transferred into a 50 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (teflon) liner stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 190 °C for 8 h in an oven, followed by being cooled down to room temperature naturally. The as-prepared CQDs solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and purified through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 3000) against pure water to remove the unreacted TSPP and CA. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried to collect dry CQDs.

The nanosensor solution was prepared by dispersing CQDs in PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) with an identical concentration of 0.1 mg/mL. The Cu2+ detection experiment was conducted by mixing varying concentrations (0–2 μM) of Cu2+ solutions, respectively, with 1 equiv. of the nanosensor solution. After standing for a certain time, fluorescence spectra of the nanosensor were collected. The equilibrium time was optimized by dynamically scanning the red fluorescence of nanosensor in the presence of 1 nM, 10 nM and 100 nM of Cu2+, respectively, with a time resolution of 10 s. The selectivity experiment was conducted by mixing various metal ion solutions with 1 equiv. of the nanosensor solution. For simulating the biological media, the concentrations were set at 140 mM for Na+, 5.0 mM for K+, 2.5 mM for Ca2+, 1.0 mM for Mg2+, 20 μM for Zn2+, Fe2+, 1.0 μM for Fe3+, Mn2+, Cr3+, and 0.5 μM for Ni2+ and Co2+. For studying the anti-interference capability of the nanosensor to biological environment, 1 μM of Cu2+ solution was mixed with 1 equiv. of nanosensor solution spiked with potential interferences including biomolecules (11 mM for Glu, 5 mM for GSH, 3 mM for Cys and His, 0.2 mM for BSA) or Fe3+ (1 μM), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cytotoxicity test of the nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Both HeLa cells and HepG2 cells were cultured in 10 % FBS supplemented DMEM at 37 °C with 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere. Cytotoxicity of the nanosensor was investigated using HeLa cells as the model. The viability of HeLa cells was measured by MTT assay. Briefly, 20 μL of nanosensor solutions with varying concentrations were added into the DMEM after the cells were cultured for 24 h. The cells were further incubated for 10 h in the presence of nanosensor. Afterwards, the cells were washed 3 times with PBS buffer to remove the nanosensor spiked culture media, and 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added into each well and the mixture was further incubated for 4 h. Finally, 150 μL of DMSO was added into each well after the medium was discarded and the absorbance in the wells was measured at 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular Cu2+ quantification and uptake kinetics measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake of nanosensor was studied by incubating HeLa cells with 0.05 mg/mL of nanosensor for varying time. After washing 3 times with PBS buffer, the fluorescent images of the cells were taken and the fluorescence intensities (20 cells per sample) were analyzed by ImageJ software.

For imaging cellular Cu2+, the HeLa cells were pre-treated with 0.05 mg/mL of nanosensor for 1 h. Subsequently, the nanosensor spiked culture media were replaced by those spiked with various amounts of Cu2+, respectively. After incubating for 4 h, the cells were washed 3 times with PBS buffer and their fluorescent images were taken. For quantifying the internalized Cu2+ with the nanosensor, the changes of fluorescence intensity ratios were correlated with the Cu2+ detection results via standard ICP-MS. Detection of Cu2+ concentration in each cell by ICP-MS was performed according to the reported method [46]. Briefly, the cells were detached using 0.25 % trypsin and re-suspended in 1 mL of PBS buffer, followed by the cell density estimation using hemocytometers. Finally, the cells were digested using 2 mL of freshly prepared aqua regia (HCl/HNO3 = 3:1). The total content of copper is then detected, and the concentration of internalized Cu2+ is achieved by subtracting the endogenous Cu2+, which was obtained from the untreated cells. For measuring the Cu2+ uptake kinetics by different cell lines, HeLa and HepG2 cells were sequentially treated with nanosensor (0.05 mg/mL, 1 h) and Cu2+ with different concentrations (0−1000 nM) according to the aforementioned procedure. A 30 min incubation time was adopted for calculating the initial rates and uptake kinetics. Afterwards, cells were washed 3 times with PBS buffer and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, then the fluorescence images were taken. The amounts of internalized Cu2+ within 30 min were calculated according to the cellular Cu2+ quantification results.

The fluorescence spectra and lifetime measurements were performed using an FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh instruments, UK) with the excitation wavelength at 420 nm, unless especially stated. The fluorescence decay data were fitted to appropriate exponential model [47], followed by estimating the average fluorescence lifetime according to the reported equations. The absorption spectra of CQDs (0.5 mg/mL in PBS buffer) were obtained with a UV-1780 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The absolute quantum yield (QY) of CQDs were estimated following the method reported elsewhere [43], using rhodamine 6 G (QY: 95 % in ethanol) as standard. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of CQD powder were performed on an AXIS UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) and attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, German). KBr disks containing the powder samples were used for FTIR measurements, while nanosensor solutions (5 mg/mL) containing 10 μM of Cu2+ or Fe3+ were used for ATR-FTIR measurements. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were acquired with a JEM-2100 instrument (JEOL, Japan). Diameters of the CQDs were estimated by measuring the size of 50 particles with Nano Measurer software. The Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was conducted on a CHI-660D electrochemical workstation, with a typical three-electrode system consisting of a gold working electrode, an Ag/Ag+ reference electrode, and a platinum wire counter electrode. The electrolyte was 0.1 M KCl in pure water. Cell imaging experiments were performed on a LSM710 laser confocal microscope (Zeiss, Germany). Excitation wavelength of laser was 405 nm, and the emissions at 500−540 nm and 660−700 nm were collected. Quantifying of intracellular Cu2+ was conducted on an iCAP RQ inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Customizable preparations of dual-emissive CQDs via successive polymerization and carbonization process
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were facilely prepared by hydrothermal treatment of TSPP and CA. For revealing the formation mechanism, as well as customizing the fluorescence property of the CQDs, a series of TSPP-to-CA molar ratios ranged from 1:5 to 1:250 were adopted in the synthesis. The products were characterized in detail. TEM images in Fig. 1 A show that spherical shaped CQDs with high crystallinity are obtained if the TSPP-to-CA ratios are less than 1:20. The crystalline spacing lattice is 0.22 nm, close to the hexagonal pattern of graphene [48]. The diameter statistic shows that the particle size gradually decreases from 3.4 ± 0.4–2.2 ± 0.3 nm, along with the reduction of TSPP-to-CA ratio from 1:50 to 1:250. Nevertheless, large-sized polymer with some slightly carbonized sites and CQDs/polymer mixture, instead of discrete CQDs, are observed at the ratios of 1:5 and 1:20, respectively. Since CA is much more easily carbonized than TSPP, it is considered that formation of CQDs follows the successive two-step condensation polymerization and carbonization process, as is the case for CQDs synthesized from monoethanolamine and CA [49]. In the first stage, esterification of carboxyl group of CA and sulfonic group of TSPP, couples with the intermolecular esterification of CA, give rise to the production of large batches of hyper-branched polymer (Fig. S1). The second stage is mainly involved with the dehydration and carbonization of CA units in the polymer, producing CQDs with high uniformity and mono-dispersity. The morphology results clearly verify this mechanism: when CA is not sufficient, polymerization occurs exclusively but the polymer is difficult to be carbonized, because of the strong π-π stacking among the aromatic TSPP units. Upon increasing the amounts of CA, stacking of TSPP units can be sterically hindered, thus facilitating the carbonization under the same conditions. Besides, more carbonization sites are introduced with the increase of CA amount, lowering the CQD particle size consequently.

Not only the morphologies, but also the optical properties of CQDs support the claimed formation mechanism. As can be seen from Fig. 1B, the uncarbonized polymers exhibit weak green fluorescence and strong red fluorescence (EX = 420 nm). While for CQDs, well-resolved green and red fluorescence can be obtained. The possibility of red fluorescence originating from free TSPP is excluded because TSPP is not excitable by EX of 420 nm. Contrarily, CQDs only emit green fluorescence if the EX is 380 nm, which is an excellent wavelength for exciting TSPP (Fig. S2). With the increased amount of CA, green fluorescence of the CQDs is enhanced and blue-shifted, while red fluorescence is gradually suppressed. Evolution of the fluorescence implies that the green and red fluorescence results from the band-gap exciton recombination in the carbon core and trap-level state introduced by TSPP residue, respectively, and sets the basis for customizing the optical property of CQDs. At the same time, QY of CQDs can be improved along with the increased amount of CA, owing to the decrement of surface traps. The absolute QYs are 20.7 %, 23.4 % and 35.9 % for CQDs prepared with TSPP-to-CA ratios of 1:50, 1:100 and 1:250, respectively. Besides, the blue-shift of the green fluorescence is ascribed to the cutting down of the particle size, which is in conformity with the size tunable emission property of semiconductor quantum dots. The absorption spectra (Fig. 1C) show similar tendency with that of the fluorescence. For the products with TSPP-to-CA ratios of 1:5 and 1:20, both Soret-band (414 nm, 434 nm) and Q-band (590 nm, 644 nm) absorption peaks of TSPP appear on the spectra, suggesting the existence of uncarbonized polymer, in which TSPP is the main light extinction center. However, for the products with elevated CA amount, the absorption peak centered at 340 nm, which belongs to n−π* transitions at the edge of the carbon lattice [50], is greatly enhanced. Instead, the absorbance ranged from 400 to 500 nm, which is attributed to the transitions of surface defects, gradually decreases. The result illustrates that the carbon core is predominantly responsible for the absorption property of CQDs.

For clarifying their surface compositions, the products synthesized with inadequate CA, appropriate CA, and largely excessive CA, were further characterized by XPS and FTIR measurements. As shown in Fig. 1D, the N 1s signal is weakened along with the TSPP-to-CA ratio decreasing from 1:5 to 1:250. Accordingly, the nitrogen atom percentages are 4.35 %, 3.56 % and 2.27 % for the samples with precursor ratios of 1:5, 1:50 and 1:250, respectively (Table S1). High-resolution C 1s and N 1s spectra particularly emphasize the bond features of the elements (Fig. S3). Similar peak fitting results of C 1s spectra were obtained for these samples: CC bond at 284.7 eV, CN bond at 285.6 eV, CO bond at 286.5 eV, and CN/CO bonds at 288.9 eV [51]. The N 1s spectrum is resolved into three bonds at 399.6, 400.4, and 401.5 eV, which are assigned to the graphitic NC, pyrrolic NH, and NC bonds, respectively. The presence of graphitic NC indicates that a portion of TSPP has taken part in the carbonization reaction. Besides, the pyrrolic nitrogen is linked to the structure of the original TSPP precursor, demonstrates that the molecular structural units of TSPP are still observable in the resulting CQDs. From the perspective of a nanosensor, the TSPP residue is pivotal for hosting Cu2+, as will be discussed in 3.4. FTIR spectra in Fig. 1E confirm the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups brought by CA, and amino and phenyl groups brought by TSPP, on the uncarbonized polymer. However, for highly carbonized CQDs with largely excessive CA, the amino and phenyl groups nearly disappear, which is in good agreement with previously discussed results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric Cu2+ detection with ultra-high sensitivity
ce:section.ce:para:
Prior to Cu2+ analysis, responsiveness of CQDs prepared with different precursor ratios was investigated by adding 0−50 nM of Cu2+. It is found that CQDs prepared with the TSPP-to-CA ratio of 1:50 reveal the largest net change and descending slope of red-to-green intensity ratio (Figure S4), ascribing to their most abundant Cu2+ binding sites (TSPP residue). Therefore, these CQDs, which exhibit a primary red-to-green intensity ratio of 2.2:1, were customized as the Cu2+ nanosensor for their most remarkable ratiometric fluorescent response and sensitivity. The final concentration of nanosensor was kept as 0.05 mg/mL. The response time was defined as 10 min, which was determined by measuring the time-dependent red fluorescence quenching of the nanosensor in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ (Fig. S5). The rapid response enables their application for real-time Cu2+ detection in living cells. In PBS buffer, the nanosensor exhibits ratiometric fluorescent response toward Cu2+, as shown in Fig. 2 A. With the increase of Cu2+ concentration, the red fluorescence ( I
682) is gradually quenched, whilst the green fluorescence ( I
505) remains constant. The average lifetimes for both green and red fluorescence are insignificantly changed from the fitted decay curve (Fig. S6) and corresponding calculations (Table S2), implying a static fluorescence quenching mode rather than a dynamic one. The relationship between the red-to-green intensity ratio ( I
682/ I
505) of nanosensor and the Cu2+ concentration is depicted in Fig. 2B. The I
682/ I
505 value shows a sharply linear decline in the Cu2+ concentration ranged from 0 to 50 nM, in which dynamic Cu2+ detection is achievable according to the regression equation of I
682/ I
505 = -0.029 C
Cu2+ + 2.19 ( R
2 = 0.983). Strikingly, the obtained limit of detection (LOD) is 37 pM following the 3σ criteria. Among all Cu2+ sensors listed, the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor from the current work reveals the highest sensitivity (Table S3), primarily ascribing to the avidly binding between Cu2+ and TSPP residue on the nanosensor. Besides, the ratiometric fluorescent response of the nanosensor facilitates visual detection by displaying obvious color changes. As can be seen from the inset photographs, the nanosensor solutions display Cu2+-dependent color changes from yellow to green, which can be easily distinguished by naked eyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and anti-interference capability of the nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
High specificity and anti-interference capability of the nanosensor are the essential requirements for in-situ Cu2+ detection. For evaluating the performance of proposed nanosensor, we measured the fluorescent response of the nanosensor by adding different metal ions into the solution. As shown in Fig. 3 A, remarkably, only the presence of Cu2+ quenches the red fluorescence of the nanosensor, producing the ratiometric fluorescent response. No emission/ratiometric changes are observed for other ions in biological media such as abundant ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Mg2+) and trace ions (Fe2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cr3+). The exception is observed for Fe3+ because both the green and red fluorescence of the nanosensor is quenched. However, the fluorescence ratio of the nanosensor keeps unchanged since the dual emission bands are simultaneously quenched by the same extent of 55 %. Therefore, Cu2+ detection with high specificity is achievable, benefited from the self-correction effect of the nanosensor. The anti-interference capability is further emphasized by the finding that the nanosensor exhibits an identical I
682/ I
505 (0.3) in the coexistence of other biological compositions (Fig. 3B). For the nanosensor pre-quenched by Fe3+, the red fluorescence can be further quenched by Cu2+

(Fig. S7), showing a red-to-green fluorescence ratio identical to the Cu2+ detection with untreated nanosensor. These results demonstrate that the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor offers excellent selectivity and accuracy for Cu2+ sensing in complex biological relevant systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Signal transduction mechanism of the nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The interesting fluorescent response of the nansensor to Cu2+ has prompted us to reveal that through what mechanism the red fluorescence is selectively quenched. Interaction of nanosensor with Cu2+ was investigated by speculating the complexing mode of nanosensor-Cu2+, as well as determining the electron or energy transfer path in the complex. For providing the insight view behind its superior interference resistance, interaction between the nanosensor and Fe3+ was investigated in parallel. As shown in Fig. 4 A, Cu2+ is considered to be incorporated into the porphyrin residue of the nanosensor via forming N-Cu2+ bond [27]. Unlikely, Fe3+ is specially coordinated by hydroxyl groups on nanosensor, which has been widely used for the detection of Fe3+ [52]. The site-selective coordination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is confirmed by ATR-FTIR and absorption measurements (Fig. S8). Compared with the nanosensor, new vibration peak at 1004 cm−1 appears on the ATR-FTIR spectrum of nanosensor-Cu2+ complex, representing the metalation of TSPP residue by Cu2+ [53]. Simultaneously, the intensified absorbance at 480 nm also indicates the insertion of Cu2+ into the surface traps of nanosensor. For nanosensor-Fe3+, vanish of the ATR-FTIR peak at 1170 cm−1 and enhancement of absorption intensity in the range of 300−400 nm prove the coordination of Fe3+ by hydroxyls [54,55]. This site-selective coordination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ provides persuasive explanation for that the nanosensor is capable of excluding the interference from Fe3+.

Formation of the nanosensor-ion complexes facilitates efficiently electron or energy transfer from the nanosensor to ions. Since there is no spectral overlap between the fluorescence of nanosensor and the absorbance of Cu2+ or Fe3+ (Fig. S9), mechanism of FRET is thus excluded. Conceivably, the unfilled d orbitals of Fe3+ and Cu2+enable them to efficiently extract electrons from the photo-excited nanosensor. However, different electron transfer paths are assumed since quenching of green fluorescence depends on the type of ion, while quenching of red fluorescence is independent. In order to certify the conjecture, we calculated the energy levels of the nanosensor and further studied their alignment with those of the metal ions. From the CV curve (Fig. S10), the onset potentials of oxidation for nanosensor are 0.55 V and 0.67 V, whilst the onset potentials of reduction are -0.81 V and -1.09 V. The corresponding band-gap levels and trap-levels were then calculated according to the reported method [56], and are depicted in Fig. 4B. The recombination of photo-excited electrons on the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and holes on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) leads to a radiative green emission. Due to the introduction of trap-level in the band-gap of nanosensor, the electrons and holes are trapped and their following recombination releases the photons with the wavelengths around 682 nm. For Cu2+, the energy level is -4.2 eV [57], which is slightly lower than the trap-level of the nanosensor, indicative of the electron transfer process from the trap-level of nanosensor to Cu2+ and resulting in the red fluorescence quenching. Compared to that of Cu2+, the energy level of hydroxyl group coordinated Fe3+ is situated between the LUMO and trap-level of the nanosensor [52]. Therefore, the electron transfer path is from the conductive band of nanosensor to the d orbital of Fe3+, leading to the non-radiative exciton annihilation of the nanosensor and suppression of both green and red fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging and quantifying Cu2+ in living cells
ce:section.ce:para:
After confirming its analytical performances, the nanosensor was applied for sensing cellular Cu2+. Prior to Cu2+ imaging, the cytotoxicity of the nanosensor was evaluated by the MTT assay. The result reveals that the nanosensor has negligible effect on HeLa cell viability in 10 h, featuring low toxicity (Fig. S11). Cellular uptake of the nanosensor was investigated by incubating the cells with 0.05 mg/mL of nanosensor at varying times. By recording the green-channel (G-ch) and red-channel (R-ch) ﬂuorescence in cytoplasm, the accumulation of the nanosensor becomes saturated within 1 h (Fig. S12). For evaluating the applicability of the nanosensor for intracellular Cu2+ imaging and quantifying, HeLa cells spiked with nanosensor were further treated with different amounts (0−1000 nM in culture media) of Cu2+ for 4 h. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the native cells exhibit a red-to-green fluorescence intensity ratio ( I
R/ I
G) of 2.1:1, which is close to that for the nanosensor in solution. For the Cu2+-treated cells, the G-ch fluorescence remains stable, while the R-ch fluorescence is drastically quenched as the concentration of Cu2+ increases. Values of I
R/ I
G decrease to 1.8:1, 1.1:1, 0.4:1 and 0.2:1, respectively, for 10, 100, 500, and 1000 nM Cu2+ treated cells (Fig. 5B). As a result, the merged cell images display continuous fluorescence color change from orange to green.

With the purpose of correlating the ratiometric fluorescence changes and the amounts of internalized Cu2+, the contents of Cu2+ in living cells were measured by ICP-MS (Table S4). As the control, endogenous concentration of Cu2+ is determined to be 0.16 fmol/cell. By treating the cells with increased amounts of Cu2+, its concentration in single cell is remarkably improved, indicating the exogenous Cu2+ is internalized by the cells in the dose-dependent manner. Fig. 5C shows the relationship between the I
R/ I
G and the net change of Cu2+ amounts (Δ N
Cu2+), it is found that the fluorescence ratio of the nanosensor is linearly correlative to the internalized Cu2+ amounts with a coefficient of 0.998. These results clearly demonstrate the feasibility of quantifying intracellular Cu2+ using the proposed nanosensor, following the regression equation of I
R/ I
G = -0.16Δ N
Cu2+ + 2.07.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of Cu2+ uptake kinetics using the nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para:
To test the copper uptake kinetics is of great significance for studying the complex absorption-efflux process, as well as exploring the pathomechanisms of copper deficiency or poisoning. To the best of our knowledge, there is no such a type of probe or sensor which has been developed for measuring the Cu2+ uptake kinetics by living cells due to the rigorous requirements of sensitivity, accuracy and responsive-rapidness. To this end, the proposed nanosensor is further challenged to measure Cu2+ uptake kinetics relying on its intracellular quantifying capability. To demonstrate the effect of concentration on the initial rates of Cu2+ uptake, and to simultaneously compare the kinetics between different cell lines, Cu2+ with the concentration range from 0 to 1000 nM were spiked into the culture media of HeLa and HepG2 cells. As shown in Fig. 6 A, fluorescence intensity of R-ch is suppressed in both HeLa and HepG2 cells after 30 min incubation, ascribing to the across membrane transport of Cu2+. However, the fluorescence quenching efficiency depends not only on the Cu2+ amount in the media, but also on the cell type. The evolution of I
R/ I
G clearly shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio changes more sharply for HepG2 cells than that for HeLa cells (Fig. 6B). According to the aforementioned equation, the intracellular contents and the average uptake rates of Cu2+ are therefore obtained and depicted in Fig. 6C. For HepG2 cells, a maximum uptake rate ( V
max) of 0.35 fmol/min·cell is achieved in the presence of 800 nM Cu2+, the K
m value (concentration at which the rate is V
max/2) is 100 nM. In comparison, the Cu2+ uptake by HeLa cells is less efficient owing to its lower V
max of 0.29 fmol/min·cell and higher K
m of 200 nM. The results indicate that, on one hand, hepatic tissue can absorb Cu2+ more avidly than other tissues when being exposed to the consistent plasma environment. On the other hand, lower extracellular Cu2+ concentration is needed for hepatic tissue than the others when same amounts of Cu2+ is required by the organs. The findings obtained with the nanosensor show excellent agreement with those based on biological models [58], indicative of its applicability for measuring Cu2+ uptake kinetics in living systems.

By taking advantage of its unique structural effect, TSPP is hydrothermally treated with CA to prepare dual-emissive CQDs, which show customizable properties by controlling the molar ratio of TSPP-to-CA in the successive polymerization and carbonization process. Relying on the specific fluorescent response, the CQDs are successfully used as fluorescent nanosensor for Cu2+ detection at physiological pH condition with evident responsive-rapidness and ultra-high sensitivity. More importantly, the nanosensor exhibits anti-interference capability via distinct ratiometric fluorescence. Being endocytosed by living cells, the nanosensor shows negligible effect on cell viability, featuring with low toxicity. Together, the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor enables Cu2+ imaging and reliable quantification in living cells, based on the strict linear correlation between fluorescence intensity ratio and the amount of Cu2+ detected from ICP-MS. The practicality of the nanosensor is further exemplified by measuring cellular uptake kinetics of Cu2+. Advancing the TSPP derived CQDs represents a new trend in sensor/probe designs for biological applications, including sensing of physiologically vital compositions and their metabolisms.

Yuqian Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Farzad Seidi: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Chao Deng: Methodology, Investigation. Ruyi Li: Investigation, Data curation. Tingting Xu: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Huining Xiao: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuqian Liu received his Ph.D. in biomedical engineering from Southeast University (China) in 2019. He is currently a lecturer in College of Light Industry and Food Engineering at Nanjing Forestry University (China). His current research interest focus on the design and fabrications of 0-dimensional (semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots), 1-dimensional (cellulose nanocrystal), and bulk material (cellulose paper, hydrogel) based fluorescent sensing systems for biosensing, bioimaging, as well as food safety evaluations.

Farzad Seidi obtained his PhD in Polymer Chemistry at the Sharif University of Technology (Iran) in 2011. In July 2016, he joined the newly established Vidyasirimedhi Institute of Science and Technology (VISTEC) in Thailand as a team leader in the research group led by Prof. Daniel Crespy. Currently, Farzad Seidi is an Associate Professor at Nanjing Forestry University in China. Prof. Farzad Seidi is a skilled polymer chemist with over 50 peer‐reviewed journal papers with high impact (including Chemical Reviews and Progress in Polymer Science). He is an expert in the synthesis of functional polymers and hydrogels through various polymerization methods.

Chao deng got his Ph.D. in Nonwoven Materials and Engineering from Donghua University (China) in 2020. He was a visiting Ph.D. student in School of Materials, The University of Manchester (UK) between 2017−2018. Now, he is a postdoctoral staff in College of Light Industry and Food Engineering at Nanjing Forestry University (China). His current research work is about structural control and characterization of fibrous (pulp and cellulosic fibre) materials, chemical modification of functional cellulosic materials, and green-based packaging materials.

Ruyi Li is a master student in Nanjing Forestry University (China). His undergraduate major was light chemical engineering, and received his B.S. in 2019. Afterwards, he started his research career in the College of Light Industry and Food Engineering at NJFU. His is interested in designing new sensing and labeling platforms based on cellulose nanocrystals and carbon quantum dots.

Tingting Xu received her Ph.D. in biomaterial (2018) from the Nanjing Tech University (China). She is currently a lecturer in College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University (China). She has strong experience on the preparation and application of biomaterials. Her current research interests include development and utilization of biosensors and drug carriers.

Huining Xiao, a Professor in Chemical Engineering at University of New Brunswick in Canada and the Fellow of Canadian Academy of Engineering (FCAE), obtained his Ph.D in Chemical Engineering at McMaster University in Canada in 1995. Before joining the UNB in 2001, he was a lecturer at the University of Manchester (formerly UMIST) in the UK from 1996 to 2001. He had actively involved in the research supported by Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada (NSERC) and Atlantic Innovation Fund (AIF). He was the Theme leader for two NSERC strategic networks: Sentinel‐Bioactive Paper and Innovative Green Wood Fibre Products Network. He has demonstrated the excellent track record in training high‐qualified personnel (HQPs). Prof. Huining Xiao has published over 275 SCI peer‐reviewed journal papers. His key research interests include responsive polymers and hydrogels, star polymers and hydrogels as drug carriers or vectors for gene delivery, functional‐modified cellulose fibres, antimicrobial polymers and nanoparticles; bioadsorbent, bionanocomposites and green‐based packaging and foam materials.
